coredata.dc:title: Abstracting photogenerated holes from covalent triazine frameworks through carbon dots for overall hydrogen peroxide photosynthesis
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2024 Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, the Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, the Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Ren, Weijie
Li, Ning
Chang, Qing
Wu, Jie
Yang, Jinlong
Hu, Shengliang
Kang, Zhenhui
coredata.dc:description: Owing to the rapid recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs with strong Coulomb interactions, the photocatalytic activity of metal-free conjugated polymers is often unsatisfactory. This article reports a simple method for incorporating carbon dots (CDs) into highly crystalline covalent triazine frameworks (CTFs) by directly heating a pretreated mixture of 1,4-dicyanobenzene, CDs, and alkali metal salts in air. The resultant photocatalyst exhibits a H2O2 production rate, solar-to-chemical conversion efficiency, and apparent quantum yield of 2464 μmol h–1 g–1, 0.9% at full spectrum, and 13% at 500 nm, respectively, surpassing most reported photocatalysts. The results of this study reveal that CDs can serve as hole extractors to efficiently drive exciton dissociation and can offer active sites for water oxidation reactions. This study is also the first to observe that alkali metal ions can interact with the carboxylic acid groups on the surface of CDs during synthesis to enhance the hole-extraction ability of CTFs, thereby accelerating photocatalytic H2O2 production. This study provides insights into the rational design of highly efficient CDs-based photocatalysts.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Covalent triazine framework
Carbon dot
H2O2 photosynthesis
Photocatalytic process
Water oxidation reaction
Photocatalyst

item-info.ce:copyright: Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, the Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Photocatalytic H2O2 production performance. (a,b) Time courses of H2O2 generation over CTFs and CDs@CTFs in different reaction solutions under simulated sunlight irradiation. (c) Comparison of the photocatalytic performances of CTFs and CDs@CTFs before and after introducing 0.1 μmol CsCl. (d) EPR spectra of CDs@CTFs and CDs@CTFs CsCl irradiated for 10 min for O2 •– detection. (e) EPR spectra of CDs@CTFs and CDs@CTFs CsCl irradiated for 10 min for ·OH detection. (f) DRIFTS spectra of CDs@CTFs irradiated for various durations.

Photocatalytic H2O2 production performance. (a) AQY of H2O2 generation over CDs@CTFs CsCl as a function of the incident light wavelength at different wavelengths. (b) Comparison of the H2O2 production performance of the proposed photocatalysts against previously reported photocatalysts. (c) Stability and durability of CDs@CTFs CsCl for H2O2 generation. (d) H2O2 evolution of CDs@CTFs CsCl during 10 h of outdoor sunlight irradiation with careful monitoring of the environmental temperature and solar intensity.

Characterization of the CTFs and CDs@CTFs. (a) FTIR spectra of the CDs@CTFs, CTFs, and DCB. (b) Powder XRD patterns of the CTFs and CDs@CTFs. (c) HRTEM images and EDS maps of CDs@CTFs CsCl. (d) XPS C 1s spectra of the CTFs and CDs@CTFs. (e) Comparison of the photocatalytic activity of the CTFs against that of the calcined CTFs and CTFs/CDs. (f) UV-vis absorption of the CTFs, CDs@CTFs, and CDs@CTFs CsCl.

Effect of metal ions on the CDs@CTFs. (a) Zeta potentials of CD solutions containing equal concentrations of different metal ions. (b) PL spectra of CD solutions before and after the addition of different metal ions. (c) Comparison of the photocatalytic activities of CDs@CTFs before and after the addition of different metal ions. (d) Comparison of the photocatalytic performance of CDs@CTFs containing different CsCl contents. (e) Zeta potentials of CD solutions containing different CsCl contents. (f) XRD spectra of CDs@CTFs containing different CsCl contents.

Interaction of alkali metal ions with CDs. (a) Schematic illustration of the combination of CDs with alkali metal ions. (b) XPS Cs 3d spectra of CsCl and CDs@CTFs CsCl. (c) FTIR spectra of the CDs before and after the addition of CsCl. (d) TGA results of the CTFs, CDs@CTFs, and CDs@CTFs CsCl. (e) FTIR spectra of the CDs@CTFs with and without CsCl. (f) Photocatalytic performance of the CDs@CTFs in different alkali metal ion solutions. (g) Photocatalytic H2O2 production performance of NCDs@CTFs with different CsCl contents.

Charge separation and transfer analysis. (a) Energy level structures of the CDs and CTFs. (b) PL spectra of the CTFs, CDs@CTFs, and CDs@CTFs CsCl. (c) Transient fluorescence spectroscopy (TFS) spectra of the CTFs, CDs@CTFs, and CDs@CTFs CsCl. (d) Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) plots of the CTFs, CDs@CTFs, and CDs@CTFs CsCl. (e) Transient photocurrent response of the CTFs, CDs@CTFs, and CDs@CTFs CsCl.

Role of CDs in charge transfer. (a,b) PL spectra of the CDs and CDs+CsCl before and after the addition of 2,4-dinitrotoluene and N,N-diethylaniline. (c,d) TFS spectra of the CDs and CDs+CsCl before and after the addition of 2,4-dinitrotoluene and N,N-diethylaniline. (e,f) Schematic illustration of the device structure used and the collected I-V curves of the CTFs and CDs@CTFs in darkness.

Photogenerated charge separation and transfer mechanism. (a) Schematic diagram illustrating the carrier separation and transfer processes in the CTFs, CDs@CTFs, and CDs@CTFs CsCl. (b,c) Calculated mean charge density distributions of the CDs and CTFs in the z-direction before and after the introduction of Cs+ ions.

head.ce:title: Abstracting photogenerated holes from covalent triazine frameworks through carbon dots for overall hydrogen peroxide photosynthesis

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Functional Nano and Soft Materials Laboratory (FUNSOM), Soochow University, Suzhou 215123, Jiangsu, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Owing to the rapid recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs with strong Coulomb interactions, the photocatalytic activity of metal-free conjugated polymers is often unsatisfactory. This article reports a simple method for incorporating carbon dots (CDs) into highly crystalline covalent triazine frameworks (CTFs) by directly heating a pretreated mixture of 1,4-dicyanobenzene, CDs, and alkali metal salts in air. The resultant photocatalyst exhibits a H2O2 production rate, solar-to-chemical conversion efficiency, and apparent quantum yield of 2464 μmol h–1 g–1, 0.9% at full spectrum, and 13% at 500 nm, respectively, surpassing most reported photocatalysts. The results of this study reveal that CDs can serve as hole extractors to efficiently drive exciton dissociation and can offer active sites for water oxidation reactions. This study is also the first to observe that alkali metal ions can interact with the carboxylic acid groups on the surface of CDs during synthesis to enhance the hole-extraction ability of CTFs, thereby accelerating photocatalytic H2O2 production. This study provides insights into the rational design of highly efficient CDs-based photocatalysts.

Carbon dots can efficiently extract photogenerated holes from covalent triazine frameworks for overall hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) photosynthesis, resulting in a rapid H2O2 production of 2464 μmol h–1 g–1 and a solar-to-chemical conversion efficiency of 0.9%.

ce:section.ce:para: This article reports a scalable method for the incorporation of CDs into highly crystalline CTFs by heating a pretreated mixture of DCB, CDs, and alkali metal salts in air (Scheme S1). This study demonstrates that the photogenerated electrons in the resultant CDs@CTF hybrid accumulated on the CTFs, while the photogenerated holes tended to sink into the CDs, thus forming spatially separated redox centers and creating dual-channel reactions, that is, concurrent ORR and WOR. Moreover, the alkali metal ions binding with CDs exhibited a strong ability to assist CDs in extracting photogenerated holes from the CTFs for the WOR. Consequently, CDs@CTFs exhibited an exceptionally efficient photocatalytic H2O2 production. A H2O2 yield rate, AQY, and SCC efficiency of 2464 μmol h–1 g–1, 13% at 500 nm, and 0.90%, respectively, which are the highest values in recently reported photocatalysts for PC systems, were achieved. More impressively, CDs@CTFs could continuously operate under natural sunlight and exhibited excellent stability after recycling and storage for 100 d.

H2O2 (30 vol%), formic acid (88 vol%), trifluoromethanesulfonic acid, DCB, ethanol, nitro blue tetrazolium (NBT), terephthalic acid (TA), NaIO3, CaCl2, MgCl2, CsCl, KCl, NaCl, CeCl3, CuCl2, 2,4-dinitrotoluene, and N,N-diethylaniline were purchased from Aladdin and used without further treatment. All aqueous solutions in the experiments were prepared using ultrapure water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDs and amino-group-modified CDs (NCDs)

ce:section.ce:para: DCB (500 mg) and a CF3SO3H solution (200 μL) were mixed and placed in a crucible. The crucible was subsequently placed a muffle furnace and heated at 250 °C for 8 h using a heating rate of 5 °C min–1. The solids were collected after the reaction and washed thrice with water and ethanol. CTFs were obtained after drying in a vacuum oven.

DCB (500 mg) and CDs or NCDs (1 mg) were added to ethanol (10 mL), followed by ultrasonication for 5 min. The resultant solution was dried at 60 °C in an oven to remove ethanol. The obtained powders were placed into a crucible containing 200 μL CF3SO3H. Subsequently, the crucible was heated in a muffle furnace at 250 °C for 8 h using a heating rate of 5 °C min–1. The solids were collected after the reaction and washed thrice with water and ethanol. CDs@CTFs were obtained after drying in a vacuum oven.

MeCl (CsCl, KCl, NaCl, CeCl3, CuCl2, CaCl2, or MgCl2) (0.1 μmol) was completely dissolved in 10 mL water to form Solution A. Concurrently, 1 mg CDs was ultrasonically dispersed in 5 mL ethanol to prepare Solution B. Solution B was then added dropwise to Solution A under vigorous stirring. Subsequently, 500 mg DCB was added to the resulting solution under stirring. The mixed solution was further ultrasonicated for 5 min and then dried in an oven at 60 °C. Next, 200 μL CF3SO3H was added to the dried powder, and the mixture was then placed into a crucible. The crucible was subsequently placed a muffle furnace and heated at 250 °C for 8 h using a heating rate of 5 °C min–1. The solids were collected after the reaction and washed thrice with water and ethanol. CDs@CTFs MeCl were obtained after drying in a vacuum oven.

The catalyst (10 mg) and water (30 mL) were placed in a 50 mL quartz beaker. Before the photocatalytic test, the resultant suspension was stirred in the dark for 30 min to achieve an adsorption-absorption balance. The entire reaction was performed under simulated sunlight with a light intensity of 100 mW cm–2. After a certain period, a small amount of the solution was removed and tested using an ultraviolet-visible (UV-vis) spectrophotometer. More specifically, 1 mL of the reaction liquid was sucked and filtered using a 0.22 μm filter to remove the solid particles. The liquid was then mixed with an as-prepared Ce(SO4)2 solution at a certain concentration, and the characteristic absorption peak at approximately 316 nm was measured and recorded to determine the H2O2 concentration.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high resolution TEM (HRTEM) were performed on a FEI Tecnai G2 F20 microscope with a field-emission gun operating at 200 kV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of different samples were obtained on a Bruker D8 ADVANCE powder diffractometer at Cu Kα with a scanning rate of 2°min–1 in the 2θ range of 10°–90°. Light absorption spectra were collected on a Shimadzu UV-2550 UV-vis spectrometer. Fluorescence spectra were recorded with a Hitachi F4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum of samples were recorded as KBr pellets with a Bruker Vertex 70 spectrometer from 4000 to 500 cm–1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was tested on the Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250Xi electron spectrometer with an Al Ka X-ray source for determining the composition and chemical bonding configurations. Time-resolved photoluminescence (PL) lifetime measurements were conducted on Hamamatsu C11367 fluorescence spectrophotometer.
Computational information is provided in the Supplementary material.

CDs@CTFs and pure CTFs were synthesized by the facile calcination of DCB and CF3SO3H with and without the addition of CDs, as illustrated in Scheme S1, to assess their photocatalytic activities for H2O2 generation. Fig. 1(a) shows the H2O2 generation performance of the pure CTFs irradiated with simulated sunlight. Fig. 1(a) indicates that the H2O2 photosynthetic rate in pure water reached 816 μmol h–1 g–1. Adding ethanol as a hole sacrificial reagent enhanced the photocatalytic H2O2 yield by a factor of 1.5 owing to the reduced detrimental charge recombination and the consequent promotion of the ORR. However, the H2O2 yield from the CTFs was suppressed by more than 99% in the presence of NaIO3 (an electron sacrificial agent) under a N2-saturated atmosphere. This indicates that H2O2 production over CTFs is governed by the O reduction pathway alone, which corresponds with previously reported findings [ 21,42 ].

The average electron numbers (n) involved in the overall H2O2 evolution for CTFs and CDs@CTFs were determined to be 1.61 and 1.93, respectively (Fig. S4), indicating that CDs@CTFs are more inclined to two-electron transfer for H2O2 generation. The intermediate products, such as ·O2 – and ·OH, were further experimentally determined. Using electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopy with 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) as the spin-trap agent, both CDs@CTFs and CDs@CTFs-CsCl exhibited strong characteristic signals assigned to DMPO-O2 •– (Fig. 1(d)) after 10 min of irradiation, indicating the generation of O2 •–. This result was also verified using the NBT method (Fig. S5(a)). The formation of O2 •– could be ascribed to single electron O2 reduction or the side reaction of H2O2 with ·OH or photogenerated holes. Moreover, the ·OH signals were observed in the EPR spectra (Fig. 1(e)) and TA detection (Fig. S5(b)). However, the EPR DMPO-·OH signals of CDs@CTFs-CsCl were stronger than those of CDs@CTFs (Fig. 1(e)), implying that more holes transferred to the surface and enabled the WOR to enhance the ·OH yield [ 48 ]. The real-time intermediates formed during the photocatalytic H2O2 formation process were identified using in situ DRIFTS. The time-dependent DRIFTS spectra of CDs@CTFs under an O-saturated condition with water steam and light irradiation (Fig. 1(f)) exhibited new vibration peaks at 885 and 1171 cm–1 that increased with the irradiation duration, which could correspond to O–O bonding and the O2 •– band, respectively [ 49 ]. The signals resulting from intermediate ·OH and O–H appeared at 1035 and 1385 cm–1, respectively, after light irradiation, suggesting the WOR processes mentioned above [ 48 ]. The H2O2 production rate markedly decreased in the presence of isopropanol as a sacrificial agent of ·OH (Fig. S6), suggesting that the ·OH + ·OH → H2O2 reaction was restrained. Therefore, the photogenerated holes from CDs@CTFs facilitated the WOR via the reactions of H2O + h+ → ·OH + H+ and ·OH + ·OH → H2O2. H2O2 was produced via a two-step two-electron dual-channel reaction over the CDs@CTFs.

The N2 adsorption isothermal test revealed that the CTFs and CDs@CTFs exhibited large Brunauer-Emmett-Teller specific surface areas of 622 and 563 m2 g–1, respectively (Fig. S13(a)). The micropore sizes of the CDs@CTFs exhibited more variation than those of the CTFs (Fig. S13(b)). Such large specific surface areas and abundant pores provide numerous active sites for the reaction substrates, such as O2 and H2O. Moreover, the combination of CDs with CTFs resulted in an increase in the UV-vis light absorption over the entire investigated wavelength range (Fig. 3(f)). Thus, the sunlight utilization efficiency was improved.

The influence of the alkali metal ion content was also investigated. Fig. 4(d) shows the H2O2 evolution activity of the CDs@CTFs as a function of the content of the investigated Cs+ ions. The H2O2 yield initially increased and then decreased with an increasing Cs+ ion content. An approximately 10 μmol L–1 Cs+ ion concentration is the optimum quantity to be added during the preparation process. The change in the zeta potential of the CDs as a function of the Cs+ ion content was further examined to better understand this phenomenon (Fig. 4(e)). The peak value also corresponds to a 10 μmol L–1 concentration. The PL spectra of the CDs revealed that an increased concentration of Cs+ ions decreased the fluorescence intensity. Consequently, excess Cs+ ions eroded the sol stability of the CDs and caused their agglomeration (Fig. S15), which influences the formation of CTFs on the CDs. Fig. 4(f) shows the XRD patterns of CDs@CTFs containing different Cs+ ion contents. The addition of Cs+ ions significantly decreased the crystallinity of the CTFs. In particular, the characteristic CTF peaks in the XRD patterns almost disappeared when excess Cs+ ions were added. Thus, the changes in the CTF crystallinity with the Cs+ ion content may be the main reason for the degradation of the CDs@CTFs photocatalytic activity. These results were also verified for other alkali metal ions (Fig. S16).

The spatial structure of CDs@CTFs can be depicted by the uniform dispersion of CDs among the stacked CTF layers. Therefore, CDs, as hole reservoirs, open a new pathway for the generation of H2O2. The band structure diagrams of the CDs and CTFs were determined using the optical band gaps and valence-band XPS spectra (Fig. 6(a) and Fig. S18) to elucidate the charge separation and transfer mechanism in the CDs@CTFs. The conduction band (CB) levels of the CDs and CTFs were –0.57 and 0.11 V versus NHE, respectively, which are positioned above the ORR (0.28 V vs. NHE, pH = 7). Their corresponding valence band positions were 1.83 and 2.14 V vs. NHE, which are below that of the WOR (1.35 V vs. NHE, pH = 7). These CD and CTF band-level structures permit electron transfer from the CDs to the CTFs and hole transfer from the CTFs to the CDs. Vitamin C was employed as an O2 •– sacrificial agent to further confirm the electron transfer process. No visible H2O2 production attenuation was observed (Fig. S19), indicating that the O2 •– formation resulted from the reaction of H2O2 with ·OH or holes rather than single electron O2 reduction. Additionally, the above experimental results and analyses indicate that the CDs not only acted as hole reservoirs but also allowed the WOR to occur on their surface. Combined with the band structure diagrams shown in Fig. 6(a), it can be deduced that the CDs@CTF photocatalyst may be a type-II heterojunction.

CDs can act as either electron donors or acceptors during light energy conversion [ 62,63 ]. The known electron acceptor 2,4-dinitrotoluene and electron donor N,N-diethylaniline were used to identify the charge transfer process between the CDs and CTFs to determine their role. As shown in Figs. 7(a) and (b) , both 2,4-dinitrotoluene and N,N-diethylaniline quenched the PL emission of the CDs solution due to static quenching contributions. When only N,N-diethylaniline was present as an electron donor, the Cs+ ions further reduced the PL intensity of the CDs. Additionally, the luminescence decay of the CDs solution was deconvoluted using a multicomponent decay function, yielding an average lifetime of approximately 6.4 ns (Figs. 7(c) and (d), and Table S2). This value decreased in the presence of quenchers. Similar to PL quenching, the Cs+ ions also shortened the average luminescence lifetime further as only N,N-diethylaniline coexisted. Cs+ ions clearly reinforced electron transfer from N,N-diethylaniline to the CDs excited with incident light, leading to a decrease in radiative recombination. Combined with the improvement of the WOR with CsCl, it can be deduced that Cs+ ions bonded with CDs endow the CDs with a high potential by reserving more photogenerated holes for accepting the transferred charges.

Additionally, hole-only devices were fabricated and their dark I-V curves were determined to estimate the hole-trapping capacity of the sample according to the equation V TFL = eNtL 2/2ԑԑ 0 (Fig. 7(e)), where V TFL represents the trap-filled limit voltage, e is the electron charge, Nt is the defect density, L is the thickness of the sample film, and ε and ε 0 are the relative dielectric constant and vacuum permittivity, respectively [ 64,65 ]. The CDs@CTFs and CTFs V TFL values were determined to be 0.16 and 0.13, respectively (Fig. 7(f)), indicating that CDs@CTFs exhibited a higher capacity to trap holes than CTFs.

Fig. 8(a) illustrates the carrier separation and transfer mechanisms. The contact between the CDs and CTFs in CDs@CTFs induce negative and positive charges on the CDs and CTFs, respectively, generating an internal electric field in the direction from the CTFs to the CDs [ 66,67 ]. The replacement of the H+ ions of the carboxyl groups on the CDs with Cs+ ions elevate the intensity of the as-formed internal electric field. Most importantly, this electric field accelerates the dissociation of the excitons formed on the CTFs by directly driving photoinduced hole migration from the CTFs to the CDs. This is beneficial for conducting the ORR and WOR via free charge carriers.

Figs. 8(b) and (c) depict the three-dimensional charge density distribution and plane-averaged charge density difference (Δρ) along the z-direction, respectively, as determined using density-functional theory (DFT) calculations (Fig. S20). Charge accumulation is shown in the green region, whereas charge depletion is shown in the yellow region. The calculated results revealed that electrons were transferred from the CTFs to the CDs via interfacial contact. Thus, a built-in electric field directed from the CTF to the CD formed after charge redistribution. In particular, the calculated results verified that this electric field can be dramatically strengthened by the combination of Cs+ and CDs, which corresponds with the experimental observations. Owing to the presence of this built-in electric field, the photogenerated holes of the CTFs were forced to migrate to the CDs when CDs@CTFs with and without Cs+ ions were exposed to light, relaxing the excitons. Therefore, the above carrier separation and transfer mechanism is robustly supported by the theoretical analysis.

A facile and scalable method for incorporating CDs into CTFs was developed in this study for highly efficient photocatalytic H2O2 production. CDs play a vital role in extracting photogenerated holes from CTFs through the internal electric field generated at their interface and act as hole reservoirs on CTFs to provide a new WOR pathway. Additionally, alkali metal ions, such as Na+, K+ and Cs+, can significantly regulate the intensity of the internal electric field by reacting with the carboxylic acid groups on the surface of the CDs during synthesis, endowing CDs@CTFs with a remarkable photocatalytic performance. Therefore, CDs@CTFs containing Cs+ ions exhibited a fast H2O2 production rate of 2464 μmol h–1 g–1 and the highest overall H2O2 production efficiency among the reported polymer photocatalysts, with a SCC efficiency of 0.9% at full spectrum and an apparent quantum yield of 13% at 500 nm, which are considerably higher than those of pure CTFs. Importantly, the CDs@CTFs were resistant to radical oxidation and possessed outstanding stability after storage for 100 d. This study provides a new design strategy for manipulating carrier separation and migration in organic conjugated polymers.
Supporting information is available in the online version of this article.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interest.
